overview: The assessment area covers the entire portfolio of activities through which we assess students’ knowledge and skills – from quick quizzes to extensive written exams and project assignments to peer review – and how AI can support, streamline, or enrich these processes. The aim is not only to measure knowledge, but also to provide valuable feedback to support further development. AI can bring automation to this process, ensure consistency of assessments, and at the same time, enable more personalized communication with students.

instructions.maturity_scoring: For each item/statement in the following table, select: [0] Not in place | [1] Partially in place | [2] Fully in place providing also clear evidence for the suggested score and owner responsible for this item. Examples for evidence and owner are provided within the table below. Note that items which are marked as Critical need to be implemented before approaching a higher maturity level (necessary/minimum conditions).

items.item: Automated evaluation of closed questions (e.g. quizzes in LMS)
items.evidence_examples: LMS records, quiz logs, faculty reports

items.item: Generation and partial evaluation of open-ended answers (essays, reflections) by AI
items.evidence_examples: Assignment logs, pilot reports, teacher feedback

items.item: Drafting personalized comments on assignments based on rubrics
items.owners: Center for the Development of Teaching Competencies

items.item: Integration of AI tools for plagiarism detection and originality verification
Analytical reports on student performance and patterns

items.evidence_examples: Assessment logs, adaptive tool usage reports

items.item: Transparency of AI- supported assessment tools

score_ranges.interpretation: AI use in assessment is limited to pilots or isolated tools (e.g., quizzes or plagiarism checkers). Processes are manual, fragmented, and lack transparency or oversight.

AI-supported assessment tools are routinely used in selected courses. Automated grading, feedback, and analytics exist but remain partially siloed. Ethical transparency is emerging, but full integration with institutional quality frameworks is pending.

AI-driven assessment is systematic, ethical, and transparent. Adaptive assessments, analytics dashboards, and feedback automation are institution-wide. Processes comply with data protection, fairness, and explainability standards, enabling continuous improvement.

build_action_plan.intro: In the early stages of implementation, it is crucial to first analyze existing assessment practices and identify the main problematic points for teachers and students. Based on a pilot study within one faculty, it is recommended to introduce AI to generate and automatically evaluate quizzes, which will free up teachers' capacities for better feedback. Once the effectiveness of these solutions is confirmed, it will be expanded to other subjects and additional teacher training will be carried out, focused on modifying and supplementing AI-generated comments so that they correspond to specific assessment goals. In the most advanced stages, it is necessary to translate the acquired knowledge into standard practices, including the creation of adaptive assessment scenarios that dynamically change the level of difficulty according to the current student results and provide deep statistical insights into common misunderstandings. Before approaching a higher level of maturity in the AI DTP area AI supported Assessment Practices, the critical items from 3.5.2 need to be fully implemented. For each unmet critical item, the necessary action needs to be specified to be performed (see table of foundational actions below).

foundational_actions.action: Implement automated evaluation of closed questions in at least one faculty and document process.
Integrate AI plagiarism detection tools into the LMS and ensure access for all relevant courses.
Ensure transparency and clear communication about AI- supported assessment tools for students and teachers.

Emerging.action: Map existing assessment processes and needs (workshop with teachers).
Pilot AI quiz generation tool in one faculty.
Allocate budget and time for initial implementation.

Established.action: Extend AI scoring of closed and semi-open responses to multiple courses.
Implement a comment drafting assistant, train teachers to review AI outputs.
Established.owners: Center for the Development of Teaching Competencies

Established.action: Integrate automatic originality verification directly into the LMS.

Enhanced.action: Monitor and optimize AI models based on student feedback.
Enhanced.owners: Center for the Development of Teaching Competencies

Enhanced.action: Create adaptive assessment scenarios that adjust difficulty in real time.
Share best practices across faculties, organize internal conferences on AI assessment.

kpis.intro: The following table presents key performance indicators (KPIs) for this area and target values required to achieve each maturity level. For each KPI, the corresponding owner should be specified.

items.name: Average reduction in teacher time to manage assignments (design, grading, feedback logistics) after AI (Evident from a survey)
Percentage of courses that use at least one AI- supported assessment feature
Student satisfaction rate with the quality of feedback (percentage of positive responses evident from a survey)
items.owners: Center for the Development of Teaching Competencies

items.name: Number of cases of plagiarism detected with AI detection
Percentage of courses/ programmes applying validated AI-assisted assessment or analytics tools
items.owners: Center for the Development of Teaching Competencies
